speaker: interviewer
text: Ludwig Weiler. This is tape eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in [INAUDIBLE] lager, this was a camp of about 6,000 or 7,000 people where we have just been replacing 1,500 people who were, unfortunately, sent back to Auschwitz for extermination with an ill transport. The people in there consisted of Polish people, Hungarians, Romanians. A mixture of the usual tapestry of European Jews-- only Jews. And we were assigned to a factory called Braubach. It's [NON-ENGLISH]-- that was a side branch of I.G. Farben-- which factory produced synthetic petrols. You know, Germany was short of oil and petrol during the war. And we-- I myself was working. I was assigned to a group who were building bunkers, concrete bunkers. So my work consisted of carrying cement bags-- at my age-- a full cement bag on my back. But as I had broad shoulders and I was quite strong, I stood up to the strain. I found that the only thing which really was sort of weakened me or I found myself very tired is when we went into a night shift. Even so, the first half of the night, it was OK. But when it came to the early hours of the morning, that's-- in daybreak is when one has got the least resistance-- I find myself tired, you know. And then during the day, obviously I slept some. But one doesn't get, during the day, the same rest as otherwise. And there were appells in between and everything. I mean, it wasn't just a work holiday camp. The conditions were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not the worst, I would say, from what I hear from other people. The-- some of the kapos behaved terribly badly and were hitting people, as they walked, with sticks for no reason at all. And sometimes they were 15-, 16-year-old boys, because they were-- it's-- it's-- it's unbelievable how, in no time, people can become inhumane, you know. It-- it-- it's a catching illness. It-- it's-- it shows that people need constant education and watch. You can't just let them go wild. Anyway, however, there were blockaltestes. I remember a-- a Polish lawyer from Warsaw. He was such a decent, nice chap who helped people and made sure if somebody wasn't quite well that he hid them. He told them, this man is today needed in the kitchen work. You know, there is two sides of the coin. There were very decent people who saved other people's lives. There were some who just, like the majority, they took care of themselves. And there were some very nasty ones. This is the-- this is life. Anyway, so we were working there. The one interesting point is the camp was no different than many others. We didn't have the cruelties of-- of that camp next to Buchenwald, Dora, where the German rockets were produced. And it was a terrible place. We knew about that. And when it came to Yom Kippur, strangely enough, the camp management, you know, the people who were in charge of the haftlinge, the-- the-- the inmates, arranged with the Germans that we can-- we can have our main meal on erev Yom Kippur, because we will not eat the next day. And then we can have something to eat in the evening, you know, which was quite tremendous considering what went on. So again, I'm trying to say that it was lucky in which camp you were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- How did you know-- I mean, how did you know when-- that it was Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were people who-- who counted the days and they knew. Look, the English dates, we-- the-- not English dates. The secular dates we knew, because anybody, even a guard or a-- there was, for instance, in that factory, there were hundreds and hundreds of forced labor workers. There was the Organisation Todt that was a labor organization who were elderly Germans, similar to the Wehrmacht, but doing work. And-- or supervising work. And some of them were quite reasonable people. They didn't hit us. Or didn't-- it happened, but not on a regular basis. And you could ask them what date it is today. You know, they-- they-- it depends who you were or what you were doing. If you helped him up-- just that he dropped the pan-- you could ask him, [GERMAN]. But people knew it. I mean, there-- there-- in every camp there was an office which was dated, events recorded, so there was not a problem. And Jews can work out if [HEBREW] is on Saturday on this date, then Tisha B'av is three weeks later, and therefore Rosh Hashana is on that date. So that was not a problem. Then I must tell you in the early part of our existence there, there was quite a lot of bombing by the Americans, daytime bombing, because it was an important industrial complex there. And the practice was that all the Germans and the forced laborers and the non-Jews were allowed to go out of the factory into bunkers. The Jews, not. Jews were led out of the-- of the factory perimeter. And we were sent down into an sandgrube, which is a sand pit. The sand pit consisted of a road approximately three, four, meters wide on the bottom, and then a sand bank going up on both sides, I would say, at least three or four stories high. So that it was literally like a sand pit where they were working it up from the bottom. It was useful from the point of view that one of the things which a lot of people got injured during the war generally is from the aerial pressure which punctures your ear drums from-- even if you were not hurt by shrapnel or something or a bomb, the air pressure could deafen you. And the-- anything. Or even a piece of metal could fall on you. So from their point of view, it was a relative safety. But we were in the open elements. And I never forget, it was a sunny autumn day when-- in-- in-- in 1944, and my brother and I and my father were out there. And we-- as I told you earlier, we got wise to the events of war, you know. And we knew that bombing always takes place when the leading plane makes a circle, a wide circle, like a cloud-- you know, like the red arrows do when they do the exercises. A wide circle, and that is the bombing target. And we were walking down there. There was no particular guards there. And once we were let down there, that was that. And I look up, and I just had time to say to my father and my brother, I touch them, I said, look up! The bombs go-- the bombs are coming here. And in this second, I don't know what reason, just out of instinct, I pushed my father and my brother forward, and they fell into the sand. And I fell on top of them, because I was-- they were bigger than me. The other way, four or five meters away, people got killed because it was really truly the place where the bombs fell. So even when you are in a camp, you can get killed by bombs. It is a matter of your destiny or your luck. Anyway, now, as winter approached, for some reason or other, they transferred us in the same area up the hill into another camp, which was opposite the railway station called Reinsdorf. I've seen that camp being described in Martin Gilbert's book. It's not-- wasn't one of the well-known camps. It had about 6,000, 7,000 people with the changes taking place, which is being disregarded. And we were there till February, 1945, when, again, we managed to get back to Buchenwald with an ill transport. Because by that time, Auschwitz was liberated by the Americans, so they couldn't take anybody back there, and we got back to Buchenwald. Circumstances were terrible by that time. Food was not available. Dysentery, illness, you name it, you couldn't compare the place to what it was. Food was practically non-existent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why had you wanted to go back-- why did you think it was a good thing to back to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father was getting a little bit weak. He got an infection, and they had to amputate a finger of his. And we felt that he won't be able to work, and it's better if we go back with-- he gets back with an ill transport, because we knew that Buchenwald was a transit camp. And if he goes, then we want to go as well. And we wheedled it out that we got in. But before we were sent back, one interesting event happened-- there's nothing terrible about it-- that on Christmas-New Year period, suddenly they decided one night they take everybody for disinfection. And one of the worst thing happened that people, due to the fact that we were not having washing facilities and bathrooms and so on, lice was very predominant. And lice, in turn, created illnesses, like typhus and so on. And we were slightly a little bit puzzled that why just suddenly like that. We knew about all this disinfection. By-- by that time we were seasoned into what was going on, and we were-- thought maybe they're doing something funny. Maybe it's another little extermination affair.

speaker: interviewer
text: But so it-- it's a small camp. Why should they do it? Maybe not.

speaker: interviewee
text: As it happens, it was a perfectly legitimate disinfection. We got our clothing cleaned. We got it back clean and dry and warm. And we had a very nice-- maybe as a Christmas present, I don't know. Or a New Year's present they give. So for a few days we felt refreshed again. Anyway, having come back to Buchenwald, and having described you what the circumstances were, we were put in the middle lager, Block 59, which mainly sort of ill people and people halfway out and halfway in this world existed. Some stronger, some weaker. And in the summer of '44 when we were in Buchenwald, we knew that there were two hospitals-- the Kleinrivier and the [INAUDIBLE]. What we didn't know was that in the same middle lager, they turn block number 61 into a killing block where they give the lethal injections. And literally, as they went around the camp, because they couldn't send back to Auschwitz people anymore, they selected the ill or not so ill people on a regular basis, and they were allowed to go into so-called hospital. That was the purpose of it. Even there the deception followed that if you're not well, you can go into hospital. And in the summer of '44, it was genuine. If you were not well, you could go into hospital. Anyway, but nobody really wanted to because it was an extreme-- you didn't know-- they said it's warmer and you get a bit more food and whatnot. You don't have to go to appell. You know, there were certain benefits. We knew people who came out of those hospital. But we didn't know-- by the end of February, about 25, 26 of February, my father was getting a little bit weak. I mean, he could have still survived, because the liberation of Buchenwald was 11 of April. But we thought he can't so much to help himself. It's better if he's in hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in he went. Then thereafter, my brother and I left from that block-- I don't know how we got ourselves out, but there were too many ill people and whatnot-- and we went back down into block 67, which was in the zeltlager in the-- the-- in the canvas blocks. And we were there. So eventually, two, three weeks later, we were talking, and my brother and I, said, look, why not go into the hospital as well? We are together with our father. Don't forget, we managed it all the way through. And it's warmer, maybe we get a bit more food. And we certainly don't go out to work. So unfortunately, towards the 21st, 22nd, 23rd, something like that, of March, we both applied when they came. You know, all you have to do is go to the front of the block and stand there till they came. And they examined you or they looked at you-- they didn't examine you-- and they said, yes or no. You have to stay in the block or you go into hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea of this-- of what went on in Block 61, unfortunately. My brother was at least in as healthy, if not better condition, than I was. They took him. They didn't take me. And that was that. Immediately after the liberation, I became aware of it. I read the records, which all sorts of false reasons were given-- pneumonia, dysentery, heart attack, whatever. It is a known fact it was conducted by a Polish non-Jew doctor who was giving all these injections, whatever. And whoever went there never came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the last time you saw your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And that was literally-- look, on the camp records, which I have got, which I got from the Yad Vashem, it's written on it 'died, verstorben, 26 of March at 3:15 in the afternoon.' And that exactly, 15:15. And the camp was liberated on the 11th of April. So you can see how close we were. And nothing major has happened except the day before the liberation-- and I must tell you that, because that is really personal. It happened to me. A day before the liberation, on the 10th of April, the Germans instructed the German political inmates to form a human wall from the bottom camp, to the zeltlager all the way up to the appellplatz, and chase all the Jews-- because the Jews were more or less concentrated in the bottom lager and a little bit in the middle lager, not hardly any on the top lager-- and chase them out of the camp to have an appell and out. I felt it was getting very close. We heard already cannon fire. You know, the sounds were getting near, so we knew any minute it can happen. And I didn't feel that strong anymore that I should go on the march or something. We knew about the marches. And all I did was create a passive resistance, which consisted of nothing else, but as they pushed us, I just sat down. I sat down there whether there was mud, water, or a stone. It didn't matter. I just sort of collapsed on the-- I sat down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, while we're still on the bottom camp, two people picked up my arm, and suddenly somebody with a big stick, a heavy timber stick which, they had, hit me over the head. I thought my head splits, but nothing serious happened. I didn't even bleed. And if I did, I didn't notice it. So I walked another few steps. By that time, I was through the barbed wire gates which separated the middle camp from the lower-- the zeltlager, the tent camp. And I said down again. And somehow or other, one of these non-Jewish German political-- either Socialist or Communist-- seemed to have taken pity on me or something and said to me in German, [GERMAN]? What's wrong with you? So I answered him, ich kann night gehen. I can't walk. He picked me up, moved me out of the line, which was totally against the rules and regulation, put me in front of block 59 on a heap of dead people, and he said two words to me-- [GERMAN]. Oh, three words, actually. [GERMAN] And with that, the man turned around and he went back and stood in the line. Because I looked up what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: These 5,000 people were taken out of the camp. To the best of my knowledge, nobody survived. Apparently a lot of them got shot in-- in-- in-- in the forest outside and eventually some were taken on a march. I have not met or heard anybody. And if there are, I don't know where they are. The other interesting Buchenwald camp story is that as it was a very old established concentration camp, the oldest in Germany, all the important Socialists and Communists were sent there, some to Dachau as well, but this was the first one. And eventually, like in all places, like in a prison, the senior prisoners become somehow the bosses or organizers what really happens in the camp. OK, the instructions and the orders came from the SS and the Germans, but what went on in detail in the camp was up to them, which could mean life or death at the time. And what has happened, the-- the-- the Germans, out of maybe gratefulness or whatever, when they liquidated the Auschwitz camp, they-- they killed a lot of people and gassed them, but the remaining people who are kapos and blockaltestes and criminals who did all the worst things, they created a transport-- they made up a transport, and they sent them to Buchenwald. It became common knowledge in February of '45, when we were there, that these people arrived from Auschwitz. We knew it. I mean, people recognized people. And it was put to the Socialist/Communist management there that these people killed people, and these people maimed people to the point that they had no chance of survival. There was a guy by-- a very tall man, a German, gentile. His name was Gustave. He was a Communist. He became, I think, quite a big mucker in East Germany afterwards. He survived. And he literally appointed a kangaroo court of these German political prisoners. They drew on maybe one or two others. I don't know exactly who they consisted of. And this kangaroo court, so to speak, went on night after night, one after the other. And if there was a single accusing person who said that I have seen that this-and-this person, this guy has either killed or even maimed beyond sort of recovery, which meant death for that person, that guy was next morning amongst the dead on the-- not the truck, but the sort of a vehicle or whatever they were hanging around where they put all the dead people. And nobody knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was, to my mind, an element of justice-- maybe rough justice-- but certainly justice carried out, because it was a reasonably organized camp. And believe you me, these people didn't deserve to live. It was absolutely nobody had any pity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody said that they-- they-- have extended and increased the number of deaths then was-- than-- then the Germans thought was necessary. And the Germans didn't care if one prisoner killed another one. They had less one to worry about, you know. But they were literally inhumane. I remember there was a gypsy blockalteste in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- Birkenau. And I knew him. I-- I didn't know what he was doing. We were there for five days. And he was one of them who they put away with. This is just the sort of thing. Anyway, now, coming back the day I just mentioned that what happened the day before the liberation. Next morning at 10 o'clock they reinstated the same method of trying to get the rest of the Jews out of the camp. And at 11 o'clock, before much was done, because it takes time to-- they line up, they give the orders, they come out of the blocks, they stand together till they are told. You know, it doesn't happen in minutes. 11 o'clock, the air raid sirens went. Everything stopped. What was known to us was that about 12 on the clock, we saw the American tanks about 500 meters outside the camp on the main road driving along. Right. What became apparent-- and a true story-- was that the Germans ran away between 10:00 and 11:00 in the morning from-- from-- from Buchenwald to Weimar. They mined most of the camp-- or all of it, I don't know for sure-- and left a few odd guards just for show, so to speak, and gave them instructions to blow up the camp. That's how precisely even at that stage, 11 of April. The war was over in less than a month. Anyway, luckily the-- there were a large group of Russian prisoners of war which were of military age, and they were used to roughing it. They were not like civilians coming out of school and then [INAUDIBLE] homes. And they got wind of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They overpowered those few guards who were left there in the intervening hours between the time the main lot of SS left and the Americans were seen to arrive. And they got a telephone call-- I don't know exactly the time-- apparently some time between 11:30, 12 o'clock, something like that, has the plan been carried out? They said yes, which meant the-- the camp in the main part has been destroyed. And that is the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was about 21,000, 23,000 people who were liberated in Buchenwald. Oh, it was a very large camp. And I never went back there, because there was-- there's nothing much one can see. And that was that. After the liberation, I unfortunately got ill. I got typhus [INAUDIBLE], which they call--

speaker: interviewer
text: After?

speaker: interviewee
text: After.

speaker: interviewer
text: Afterward.

speaker: interviewee
text: After. Literally days after. I was very lucky, because if that happens two weeks earlier, I'm finished. And I was taken into one of the hospital, the camp hospital. The illness consisted of drinking all the time, having very high temperature, swollen feet, and generally very weak. Many, many people died on that-- after the liberations and so on. And you lost your appetite. Now, this was deadly. Once you lost your appetite, that was it. Luckily, at a certain-- a week or so later, I was, for some reason or other, transferred into the SS hospital, which is a beautiful, nice hospital outside the camp. But also within the perimeter of it. And suddenly from one day to the next, I got my appetite back. I got drip fed. That was the only way. And suddenly a day in May, it looked better, you know. And I remember that the first time I went to the bathroom when I was in the SS hospital, they had a mirror. I looked in the mirror, and that was literally less than a year since I looked myself in the mirror, about 10, 11 months. And I can tell you in all honesty, I hardly recognized myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, I didn't know what I looked like, because we didn't carry mirrors on us. Who cared? Nobody wanted to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so that was that. And thank goodness I improved reasonably. Till we-- then we heard that the-- the-- the camp there is going to be vacated by the Americans and taken over by the Russians, so we organized a group of 15 people. And I was the youngest amongst them. I looked after the luggage. We got extra clothing, and, you know, everybody got a bag of something. And we set out from Buchenwald about the middle of June, going back towards Hungary. And it's-- it's-- it's-- it's interesting to note that there are a lot of books written on Buchenwald, because the Americans have well-publicized it. One of the things which happened was the people under the age of 16, or maybe 17, were taken out of the camp-- those who wanted to. If they didn't want to, didn't have to-- either to Sweden or to the south of France or to Switzerland. Whilst these governments have very kindly offered whatever, the Swiss have particularly resisted the numbers. And there was an American Jewish officer called Lieutenant Berenbaum who was in charge, and there's a whole book written about that where he describes that the Swiss literally didn't want to let in hardly anybody. And they made false-- or printed forms for 400 people. The great Switzerland can take 400 children. And when they arrived to Switzerland, the Swiss wanted to turn it back, because they said there are more people than they are prepared to take. Then the Americans, through the military, has apparently put pressure on the Swiss that they will let it be internationally known. And they are far from white, the Swiss. And then under pressure they allowed the people to come in. I didn't go to neither of these, because I wanted really to know who came back from my family. And I thought I had somebody to go back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother, we communicated through the electric barbed wire, because pieces of paper sometimes people threw. I had one aunt, my mother's brother's wife-- youngest brother who died this year-- who was together with my mother. So she survived the selection in Birkenau and was sent on to some other camp. To the best of my knowledge, to Stutthof. But I tried to find even in Yad Vashem details about Stutthof, and there-- I couldn't find anything. And I'm pretty well-informed how to look for it. Apparently very few people survived from there, accordingly. But I heard people were put out on a ship and sunk in the ice and water. Those who survived, had terrible stories about Stutthof.
